title: December 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 | Stats NZ
content:
## December 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
04 March 2022, 10:45am
December 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 provides technical users with information about our approach to measuring the impact of COVID-19 in the forthcoming release of Gross domestic product: December 2021 quarter (scheduled for 17 March 2022).
11 March 2022: We have added a section on the Use of Household Labour Force Survey and Business Employment Data composite indicator .
This paper provides information about how we are responding to ongoing measurement challenges faced due to the nature of the economic shock caused by COVID-19. This paper follows similar updates about our methodology during COVID-19 that have been published in advance of the release of gross domestic product.
Information is presented in the following sections:
- Key events in the December 2021 quarter
- Data and methodology considerations
- Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) components
Overview of sources and methods for quarterly gross domestic product: Updates and COVID-19 adjustments is an updated guide to the concepts and methods we are using to compile gross domestic product (GDP) during COVID-19 in New Zealand.

## Key events in the December 2021 quarter
The December 2021 quarter began under raised alert levels across New Zealand, with subsequent alert level changes throughout the quarter. The new traffic light system was also introduced in the quarter on 3 December 2021. Auckland was in higher alert level restrictions (alert level 3 and Red light setting) for all of the December 2021 quarter, while the rest of New Zealand was in lower alert level restrictions (alert level 2 and Orange).  This excludes Northland and Waikato regions, which moved to different alert levels throughout the quarter.
Higher restrictions meant that there were no quarantine-free travel bubbles in place over the quarter.  This follows the September 2021 quarter, which had 23 days of quarantine-free travel with Australia and 41 days of quarantine-free travel with the Cook Islands.

## Data and methodology considerations
The abrupt economic shock caused by COVID-19 and measures to contain it posed challenges to our normal methods of measuring economic activity. The measurement of some GDP(P) and GDP(E) components will be impacted by the COVID-19 restrictions over the December 2021 quarter, after starting the quarter in higher restrictions, as well as entering the new traffic light system. We will review all components in the December 2021 quarter and where appropriate, will use alternative data and methodologies in cases where conditions support it.
In previous quarters we have released the following information papers on our approach to the measurement challenges:
- September 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- June 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- March 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- December 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- September 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
- June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19: Updated
- June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19
- COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters
We have used alternative data sources in place of some of our usual data sources and methodologies since the March 2020 quarter, which was the first quarter that captured the impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic in New Zealand. This was applied in the following cases:
- for components with methods that were unable to capture the sharp changes in activity that occurred due to COVID prevention measures (for example, employment-based indicators, which were affected by the wage subsidy scheme)
- for components that experienced sharp changes in relationships between output and intermediate consumption
- for components that have a method of interpolation without a quarterly indicator.
As new data becomes available, we continue to evaluate the necessity of these adjustments. In some cases, we reverted components back to their usual quarterly method in the quarters that followed the June 2020 quarter COVID lockdown. These decisions were based on the measured differences between adjusted and unadjusted series, as well as our judgment of the relative quality of data sources. The higher level COVID-19 alert level restrictions during the September 2021 quarter necessitated the return to alternative data sources for these components. We will be again reviewing which components can revert to their usual quarterly method in the December 2021 quarter.
See Overview of sources and methods for quarterly gross domestic product: Updates and COVID-19 adjustments for a summary of the alternative indicators used. We will provide a further update on the approaches being used for GDP(P) and GDP(E) components on 11 March 2022, ahead of the release of Gross domestic product: December 2021 quarter.

## Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) components
As part of our ongoing review process, we have identified some areas where we can improve our measurement over the COVID-19-affected period, as well as implemented some improvements to our usual quarterly methodologies.

### Use of Household Labour Force Survey and Business Employment Data composite indicator
We have switched to using a Household Labour Force Survey (HLFS) and Business Employment Data (BED) composite indicator as one of our alternative indicators from the December 2020 quarter onwards. Previously, a composite indicator of HLFS and monthly employment indicator (MEI) was used. The BED series is a better-quality data source with more detail and data processing than the MEI series.
The HLFS-BED composite indicator is a quarterly estimate of activity, calculated by multiplying the number of filled jobs from the BED, by the proportion of usual hours that were actually worked from the HLFS. This is used as an alternative indicator to measure the quarterly change in labour input (total hours worked) for an industry over the COVID-19 affected quarters.
This change affects the following subcomponents from the December 2020 quarter onwards:
- motion picture and sound recording activities
- public order, safety, and regulatory services
- medical and other health care services
- residential care services and social assistance
- heritage and artistic activities
- household consumption expenditure on health
- household consumption expenditure on recreation and culture.
See Overview of sources and methods for quarterly gross domestic product: Updates and COVID-19 adjustments for a summary of the alternative indicators used in the December 2021 quarter.

### Correction to central government final consumption expenditure
We have identified and corrected an error in a deflator used in the healthcare sales component of central government final consumption expenditure (CGFCE). This error first appeared during the March 2019 quarter and has affected each subsequent quarter. Since this correction is to a deflator, this correction only impacts constant price series. No current price series are affected.
We recently discovered that an index that was used to create a composite deflator for the healthcare sales component of CGFCE was no longer available. As a result, the composite deflator that we used previously was not complete from March 2019 onwards. To correct this, an equivalent index has been produced and included from March 2019 onwards.
The impacts of this correction on published movements include small upward revisions to GDPE and CGFCE in the March 2019 quarter only.
The impacts of this correction on published levels are upwards revisions approximating $50 million (actual, constant price) for the March 2019 quarter and each subsequent quarter.
Please note that these approximate impacts are indicative only, and there may be other revisions that are introduced during the compilation of Gross domestic product: December 2021 quarter .

### Challenges with measuring low-level breakdowns of household consumption expenditure
Challenges with allocating travel credits and travel debits to household consumption expenditure (HCE) categories have resulted in increased uncertainty in the movements of HCE categories, especially in seasonally adjusted terms, since the start of travel restrictions. This particularly affects travel-related categories such as restaurants and hotels, and recreation.
See September 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 for a full explanation of measuring low-level breakdowns of household consumption expenditure.
We will continue our use of additive outliers to treat unusual data points and will be testing the December 2021 quarter series to determine where additive outliers need to be applied. Previous outliers applied to results for the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters and/or the March, June, and September 2021 quarters will be retained in the upcoming release of Gross domestic product: December 2021 quarter .

### Seasonal adjustment of travel services and the December 2021 quarter
New Zealand’s border has been closed to almost all travellers since 19 March 2020, with the exception of New Zealand citizens, residents with valid travel conditions, and those granted approval to travel.
Exports of travel services make a significant contribution to New Zealand’s economy. Prior to COVID-19 the peak travel season fell in summer. In the 2019 calendar year, March quarter exports of travel services were worth $5.5 billion, compared with around $4.0 billion in the December 2019 quarter, $3.5 billion in the June 2019 quarter, and $3.0 billion in the September 2019 quarter.
The restrictions on travel to New Zealand in place since March 2020 mean that the value of travel service exports have declined significantly to between $1.0 billion and $1.5 billion in the June 2021 quarter and in all subsequent quarters.
As we have highlighted before in previous quarters, the change from normal seasonal patterns is effectively captured in seasonally adjusted aggregate GDP(E), meaning the full seasonal impact of reduced international tourism will be reflected in total GDP(E). However, the same impact will not be reflected in lower-level components, such as exports of travel services, because the multiplicative decomposition method used in seasonal adjustment means that smaller seasonal adjustments are made to components that have fallen in size.
The December quarter is typically the start of the international tourist season; however, this usual seasonal pattern has been disrupted with the continued border restrictions due to COVID-19. This means we are expecting to see some impact on the December 2021 seasonally adjusted growth.
In GDP(P), travel-related activity is distributed across a range of industries and appears to have a softer seasonal contribution than what we observe in GDP(E).
